title: Weekly Dose of Optimism #201
gist: A roundup of hard-tech signals: Aalo Atomics became the fourth advanced nuclear company to reach criticality by a July 4, 2026 deadline, data centers are emerging as the funding engine for nuclear and gas-turbine buildout, and 1X unveiled a 25-DOF humanoid hand it claims solves robotic manipulation.

insights.claim: Aalo Atomics went critical on July 4, 2026, becoming the fourth advanced nuclear company (after Antares Nuclear, Valar Atomics, and Deployable Energy) to reach criticality ahead of a July 4, 2026 target deadline.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Confirms the advanced nuclear sector is now delivering on aggressive timelines, de-risking the technology thesis and shifting competition toward execution and manufacturing scale-up.
insights.quote: the fourth advanced nuclear company to accomplish the mission by the July 4th, 2026 deadline
insights.locator: (1) Aalo Goes Critical on the 4th of July

insights.claim: Radiant, Oklo, X-Energy, Deep Fission, and Last Energy are expected to reach criticality later in 2026 or in 2027, with commercial deployment following within a couple of years.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: A pipeline of additional advanced reactor designs approaching criticality signals a widening investable cohort beyond the first movers.
insights.locator: (1) Aalo Goes Critical on the 4th of July

insights.claim: Packy McCormick made a small personal (non-fund) investment in Aalo Atomics before interviewing its founder in 2023, at a time he believed nuclear was strategically important but not viable for venture-fund investing — a call he now admits was wrong.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Illustrates that early skepticism about nuclear's venture-investability was mistaken just three years before multiple reactors hit criticality — a cautionary example for investors dismissing capital-intensive hard-tech as un-fundable.
insights.quote: I actually wrote a small personal check into Aalo before the podcast, back when I didn't think nuclear was fund investable but that it was incredibly important. I was right on the latter but very wrong on the former.
insights.locator: (1) Aalo Goes Critical on the 4th of July

insights.claim: With four advanced reactor designs now critical and more coming, the competitive bottleneck in advanced nuclear is shifting from scientific and regulatory risk to manufacturing execution — the winner will be whoever deploys fastest, at the best price, with a product customers want.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Investors should reweight advanced-nuclear diligence toward supply chain and unit economics rather than technology risk now that criticality has been demonstrated repeatedly.
insights.quote: The field shifts from science to manufacturing.
insights.locator: (1) Aalo Goes Critical on the 4th of July

insights.claim: Data centers, not traditional utilities, are becoming the primary customers for advanced reactors, because utilities have historically been poor customers for new nuclear technology while hyperscalers will pay for speed and eventual affordability.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Reframes the nuclear investment thesis around hyperscaler procurement rather than regulated utility capex cycles — watch data center power purchase agreements as the leading demand signal.
insights.quote: One of nuclear's challenges has been that utilities are terrible customers for the product. Fortunately, they are not the main customers for these smaller advanced reactors. Data Centers are, and they will fund whoever gets them something that works fast
insights.locator: (1) Aalo Goes Critical on the 4th of July

themes.theme: Data Centers as US Industrial Demand Engine

insights.claim: Financial Times contributor Josh Zoffer argues that the data center buildout is a unique opportunity because cash-rich hyperscalers are willing to pay directly for fast, reliable industrial technology, letting the US build domestic supply chains through genuine demand rather than subsidies or tariffs.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Supports a thesis that data center capex functions as privately-funded industrial policy — a tailwind for US power and hardware supply chains without requiring government subsidy programs.
insights.quote: The data centre bonanza is a special kind of opportunity: one where deep-pocketed buyers like Silicon Valley's hyperscalers are willing to open their wallets to pay for fast, reliable access to industrial technology.
insights.locator: (2) Data centres are a crucial test of US industrial resolve

insights.claim: Data centers are dual-purpose: valuable as compute infrastructure in their own right, and also a 'turbocharged' demand engine that funds adjacent hard-tech industries such as nuclear and gas turbines.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Investors can use data center capex growth as a leading indicator for demand in adjacent energy hardware sectors like nuclear and gas turbines.
insights.quote: they're also a turbocharged way to turn on that big, beautiful American Demand engine and fund the technology that will carry us into the Age of Miracles
insights.locator: (2) Data centres are a crucial test of US industrial resolve

themes.theme: Gas Turbines for Data Center Power

insights.claim: American Turbine emerged from stealth this week building small, highly manufacturable gas turbines (including retrofitted jet engines) that can be sold in volume to data centers needing megawatts quickly, rather than the large, complex, expertise-intensive turbines that take a long time to build.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: A new entrant targeting the gas turbine supply bottleneck for data center power — worth tracking as a supplier or investment target amid a documented turbine shortage.
insights.quote: instead of very large, complex turbines that take a long time and a lot of scarce expertise to make, they're making small, highly manufacturable turbines that they can get to customers quickly
insights.locator: (3) American Turbine Comes Out of Stealth

insights.claim: Data center buyers currently pay a premium to trade turbine efficiency for speed of power delivery, but turbines should retain value even after time-to-power pressure eases, because US natural gas abundance gives it a durable cost advantage in converting gas to electricity.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Turbine manufacturing capacity built for the current data-center rush likely has a longer-term demand floor tied to cheap US natural gas, reducing boom-bust risk for turbine suppliers.
insights.quote: buyers are happy to pay for speed in lost efficiency
insights.locator: (3) American Turbine Comes Out of Stealth

insights.claim: US energy competitiveness has two components: matching China in areas it already dominates, and winning outright where the US has a structural edge, such as converting abundant domestic natural gas into power via turbines.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: A geopolitical framing for identifying where US industrial capital is likely to concentrate: natural-gas-to-power value chains rather than head-to-head competition in China's existing strengths.
insights.quote: Part of American competitiveness in the century ahead will be beating China at things China currently dominates, but an equally important part will be winning things that we can do uniquely well.
insights.locator: (3) American Turbine Comes Out of Stealth

insights.claim: Neri Oxman's company OXMAN launched 'Vigils,' a line of capes made from 3D-knitted silk textile colored by engineered bacteria that grow indigo and melanin pigments directly into the fiber, after which the bacterial cells are washed away.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: An early commercial proof point for biofabricated, grown materials as an alternative to petrochemical-based manufacturing and dyeing — a nascent space to watch for scale-up beyond fashion into broader materials.
insights.quote: whose color is not applied but grown. We engineer bacteria to produce indigo and melanin pigments, then let them work directly on the surface of a 3D-knitted silk textile.
insights.locator: (4) Vigils Uses Colors That Are Grown, Not Applied

insights.claim: 1X announced a 25-degree-of-freedom, tendon-driven robotic hand for its NEO humanoid platform that it claims achieves near-human-level dexterity, strength, safety and reliability, demonstrated performing tasks like picking up a wine glass, assembling LEGOs, and screwing in a lightbulb.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: If verified, this closes a major hardware gap in humanoid robotics (dexterous manipulation), shifting the commercialization bottleneck toward training data rather than mechanical design.
insights.quote: 25 Degree of Freedom (DOF), tendon-driven hands for the NEO humanoid platform– achieving near human-level dexterity, strength, safety, and reliability

insights.claim: 1X argues the humanoid hardware problem is now essentially solved and the remaining challenge is collecting enough real-world data to train robot capabilities — a claim that is self-serving since 1X's business model is deploying tele-operated robots into homes specifically to gather that data.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Investors evaluating 1X or peers should treat 'hardware is solved' claims skeptically and instead track data-collection scale and quality as the real gating metric for humanoid commercialization.
insights.quote: 1X argues that with these hands, the hardware problem is basically solved and what's left is gathering the data that drives the robots capabilities. It's a bit of a self-serving argument

insights.claim: 1X describes its robotic hand as an 'API to the Physical World,' framing hands as a humanoid robot's primary interface for manipulating the environment, analogous to how software APIs mediate interaction with digital systems.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: A useful mental model for evaluating humanoid robot platforms: manipulation capability may matter more for real-world utility than locomotion or perception alone.

insights.claim: The same week OpenAI released a more human-sounding voice model and 1X released more human-like robotic hands, suggesting that as AI and robotics products become more human-like along narrow dimensions, it becomes clearer they are not becoming human but are simply becoming more useful tools for humans.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: A framing for investors weighing AI/robotics anthropomorphism: near-term human-likeness gains should be read as usability improvements rather than evidence of human replacement, supporting a 'tools augment humans' investment lens.
insights.quote: the more human-like features we give the machine, the clearer it is that they are not becoming human but will be very useful to us. Long humans.
